Frontier Cockpit Team | Software Global Black Belt
Token Economy

Token management and agent design for the GitHub Copilot AI Credits era

An executive and technical briefing on the transition to usage-based AI billing, what it changes for your engineering organization, and how to operate well under it.

AuthorFrontier Cockpit Team
RoleSoftware Global Black Belt
Duration60 to 90 minutes
Date2026-05-28
Agenda

Five parts, one shared frame.

PART IThe fact, AI Credits replace per-request billing on June 1, 2026
PART IIFundamentals, what a token is and why context engineering matters
PART IIIThe real cost, model multipliers, the promotional window, the dev-bill story
PART IVAgent design, when an agent earns its cost and when a workflow is the right answer
PART VOperation, FinOps stack and a 90-day adoption roadmap
PART
I

The fact.

On June 1, 2026, GitHub Copilot stops charging per request. Tokens become the unit of cost.

The cutover date LIVE COUNTDOWN
2026-06-01

Premium Request Units are retired. GitHub AI Credits take over.

1 AI Credit equals US$ 0.01. Input, output, and cached tokens are accounted for at each model's published API rate. Cost is now tied to work performed, not to user intent.

Old unit
1 PRU
→
New unit
1 credit = US$ 0.01
Before and after

The unit of cost moved from intent to work.

Before, Premium Request Units

Cost per user intent

A chat question and an agent session counted the same way, one premium request each. Multiplier table per model. Easy to budget, but disconnected from the actual compute consumed by agentic work.

After, AI Credits

Cost per work performed

Input, output, and cached tokens accounted at API rate. A 4-hour agentic session refactoring 50 files is genuinely 100x more expensive than a single chat question. The bill now matches reality.

PART
II

Fundamentals.

What a token is, where the cost actually lives, and why context engineering is the lever.

The unit

A token is not a word. It is a fragment of a word.

01

Subword tokenization

English averages about 1.3 tokens per word. Portuguese and Spanish run 1.5 to 1.7. Code tokenizes at variable density depending on language and formatting.

02

Context window

The total tokens a model can process in a single call. Volatile, not persistent. Every turn rebuilds the window from system, tools, history, and message.

03

Tokens are billed

Every fragment in, every fragment out, every fragment kept in cache. Under AI Credits, this maps directly to dollars on the invoice.

Three token classes, three rates

Output tokens are typically 3 to 5 times more expensive than input.

Typical agentic session cost mix
Output
65%
Input
30%
Cached
5%
Class 1
Input tokens

Everything sent to the model: system prompt, tool definitions, conversation history, current message. Base cost.

Class 2
Output tokens

Everything the model generates. The premium rate. A verbose model can produce 10,000+ output tokens per turn.

Class 3
Cached tokens

KV cache reused from prior call. Typically 10% of input rate. Prompt caching is the largest optimization lever for agentic workloads.

Context engineering

Three disciplines that separate engineering teams that pay for value from teams that pay for noise.

Discipline 01 · Curation

Minimum context, maximum signal

Every token in the window is a token billed. Close unrelated tabs. Reference files explicitly with hash file syntax instead of letting the agent guess.

Discipline 02 · Caching

Cache what repeats

System prompts, codebase indexes, and large reference docs that show up turn after turn belong in prompt cache. A 50K-token system prompt at full input rate is a budget leak.

Discipline 03 · Compaction

Compact before you grow

Long sessions accumulate context rot, where quality degrades as irrelevant material piles up. Compact history into a dense summary before it bites quality and budget.

PART
III

The real cost.

Model rates, included allowances, the promotional window, and one story that frames the entire conversation.

The cost gap nobody saw on the old bill
100x

Realistic cost ratio between a single chat question and a long agentic session.

A chat question consumes a few thousand tokens. A 4-hour agent refactoring 50 files consumes millions, plus repeated tool calls. Under AI Credits, the bill reflects the actual work.

agent-session.log
$ refactor user-service across 50 files
turn 01 · input: 12,400 · output: 8,200 · cached: 0
turn 02 · input: 18,900 · output: 12,500 · cached: 11,200
turn 03 · input: 24,100 · output: 19,800 · cached: 17,400
...
turn 87 · input: 31,200 · output: 28,400 · cached: 28,900
total: 4.2M input · 2.8M output · 2.1M cached
cost: ~12,400 credits ≈ US$ 124.00
$
Per-model rates, in AI Credits per 1 million tokens

Model choice is a budgeting decision, not an aesthetic one.

Default model · output rate
0 cr
GPT-5 mini · included in the base allowance
Cost gap
7,500x
at output rate
Reserved model · output rate
7,500 cr
Claude Opus 4.7 · per 1M output tokens
Model
Input
Output
Best fit
GPT-5 mini
included
included
Daily chat, routine completions
GPT-5
125 cr
1,000 cr
Mid-complexity reasoning and code
Claude Sonnet 4.6
300 cr
1,500 cr
Long context, sustained code work
Claude Opus 4.7
1,500 cr
7,500 cr
Hard agent tasks, reserved use

Rates illustrative as of May 2026. Always validate against the official GitHub Copilot billing documentation before committing budgets.

The promotional window

June to September 2026 is a learning subsidy. Use it.

Phase 1, pre-cutover

Run the Preview Your Usage CSV report against a recent month. Project the team's spend under the new model with concrete numbers.

Until May 31
Phase 2, learning

Subsidized rates apply. Watch which models the team reaches for, which sessions burn credits, where prompt caching pays off.

Jun 1 to Sep 1
Phase 3, steady state

Full rates active. Budgets sized to projection, model policy in place, prompt caching deployed, team trained to read the bill.

From Sep 1
PART
IV

Agent design.

When an agent earns its cost, when a workflow is the right answer, and how subagents save budget.

A design decision before it is a cost decision

Most production AI is a workflow. Save the agent for what only an agent can do.

Workflow

Code calls model

Predictable cost. Easy to monitor and rollback. Right for classification, summarization, structured extraction.

e.g. extract invoice fields
Augmented

Code calls model in a loop

Moderate cost. Predictable shape, variable depth. Loop with bounded iterations and a fallback.

e.g. research with retries
Agent

Model picks tools and adapts

Variable cost. Path unknown. Right when steps depend on the data or human-level reasoning is needed.

e.g. refactor across files
Autonomous

Model spawns subagents

Unpredictable cost, can run for hours. Reserve for end-to-end work where only this delivers value.

e.g. ship full feature
$
$$
$$$
$$$$
← where 90% of production AI lives
only when nothing else delivers →
Three patterns that pay for themselves

If your team builds agents, these three are not optional.

01 · MCP

MCP servers

Standardized protocol between agents and tools. Replaces bespoke integrations. Tool definitions become reusable and cacheable.

02 · RAG

RAG over raw context

Retrieve only what the current turn needs. Stuffing 200K tokens into every call is a budget leak. Targeted retrieval pays for itself in week one.

03 · Subagents

Subagents in isolation

Main agent delegates a focused task to a subagent with its own clean context, gets back only a summary. Keeps the main context small.

PART
V

Operation.

The FinOps stack for AI workloads and a 90-day roadmap that survives contact with reality.

FinOps stack for AI

Four layers of governance that turn a usage bill into a managed asset.

Policy
Layer 4 · governance and limits
Model allowlist per team, budget per user, additional-usage policy (allowed or blocked), promotional-window plan, escalation when budget breaches.
Visibility
Layer 3 · dashboards and alerts
Billing overview dashboard, weekly usage report, model mix breakdown, top users and top sessions, anomaly alerts on credit consumption.
Discipline
Layer 2 · engineering practices
Prompt caching enabled on long prompts, RAG over raw context, subagents for focused tasks, model routing by task class, periodic compaction in long sessions.
Foundation
Layer 1 · platform and identity
GitHub Copilot Enterprise plan with proper organization structure, SSO and identity, repository-level allowlisting, Azure AI Foundry connection where it applies.
The 90-day adoption roadmap

Three phases of 30 days. Start before June 1.

Days 1 to 30, baseline

Run the usage projection. Establish the model allowlist. Train the team on what changes. Pick the pilot squad. Define the budget per user.

30 days
Days 31 to 60, instrument

Deploy prompt caching on the long prompts. Stand up the FinOps dashboard. Run a weekly model-mix review. Refine the budget based on real usage data.

30 days
Days 61 to 90, scale

Expand from pilot squad to organization. Publish the internal playbook. Connect to Azure AI Foundry where governance demands it. Lock the steady-state policy.

30 days
Closing

Tokens reward engineering discipline.

Building the future of software development with AI and Agentic DevOps.

Published 2026-05-28
.github/copilot-instructions.md · start here Monday
# Model policy
Default model: GPT-5 mini (included)
Premium models: only for hard tasks

# Context discipline
Reference files with #file:path explicitly
Use @workspace only when search-wide is needed

# Caching
System prompts cached after first turn
Long reference docs always cached
Use ← → para navegar · O overview · N notas
Speaker notes
Visão geral
Clique em um slide · Esc para fechar
